title: Open source AMS/RF/digital simulation (don't pay more)
gist: An independent developer, aided by Claude, has assembled an open-source mixed-signal/RF/digital simulator from iverilog, nvc, yosys and Xyce aiming to displace commercial EDA simulators, giving away the base tools (including a PLL generator and a CDC-bug functional simulator) and monetizing specialist modeling.

insights.claim: A new open-source mixed-signal simulator has been assembled by combining the iverilog, nvc, yosys and Xyce open-source tools into a single integrated flow.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Signals maturing open-source EDA components can now be stitched into a working AMS flow, a long-term threat to commercial simulator vendors' pricing power.
insights.quote: It's a mash of iverilog, nvc, yosys and xyce
insights.locator: Thanks to Claude I got to build

insights.claim: The stated goal of the project is to replace incumbent commercial simulators with a tool that both works properly and performs better, with current benchmark numbers reflecting the free analog simulators.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A performance-and-correctness pitch against commercial EDA simulators is the wedge to watch; verify via the published perf benchmarks before assuming displacement risk to incumbents.
insights.quote: The goal is to replace all old crufty commercial simulators with something that works properly and performs better
insights.locator: The goal is to replace all old crufty

insights.claim: The sv2ghdl project is a translator that converts SystemVerilog DUT code into gHDL-compatible VHDL, enabling SystemVerilog designs to run through open-source VHDL simulation.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: SystemVerilog-to-VHDL translation removes a key interoperability barrier that keeps design teams locked to commercial tooling.
insights.quote: Translator for SystemVerilog DUT code to gHDL compatible VHDL code.

insights.claim: The developer credits the AI assistant Claude with enabling completion of a simulator project he had wanted to build roughly 15 years earlier.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A concrete example of AI coding assistants lowering the barrier for individuals to build complex EDA infrastructure once requiring large teams.
insights.quote: Thanks to Claude I got to build the simulator I wanted to build ~ 15 years ago.
insights.locator: Thanks to Claude I got to build

insights.claim: The developer is giving away the base simulator for free and intends to monetize specialist modeling services rather than the core tool.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A classic open-core EDA business model; the monetizable value sits in bespoke modeling, not the free simulator, shaping where any commercial upside accrues.
insights.quote: The money-making proposition (for me) is in this specialist modeling, so I'm letting people use the base simulator for free

insights.claim: The developer offers to tune the tools for specific customer needs on request, indicating a services-and-customization revenue path around the free tooling.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Confirms the go-to-market is consulting/customization-led, a model that scales with people rather than software licenses.
insights.quote: YMMV, get in touch if you want it tuned for something specific.

insights.claim: The stat-sim tool is claimed to be the only way to catch clock-domain-crossing (CDC) bugs using functional simulation.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A unique CDC-via-functional-simulation capability, if validated, targets a notoriously hard verification gap and is the intended commercial hook.
insights.quote: this tool is the only way to catch CDC bugs with functional simulation

insights.claim: The kestrel project is a free PLL generator built on TCI's expired patents.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Using expired patents to give away IP-heavy PLL generation illustrates how lapsed IP can be commoditized into free tooling, eroding a paid-IP moat.
insights.quote: PLL generator based on TCI's expired patents.
